03 Projects
Customer Churn Prediction
GitHub Live Demo
✓ 0.821 ROC-AUC 79% churn recall
  • Built an end-to-end ML platform to predict telecom customer churn, analyze customer segments, and generate targeted retention strategies
  • Built a LightGBM classification pipeline with feature engineering, SMOTE balancing, and hyperparameter tuning
  • Added K-Means customer segmentation and SHAP-based explainability to surface churn drivers and business insights
  • Self-hosted the live demo on AWS EC2 with an Nginx reverse proxy for improved uptime, response latency, and production-grade reliability
  • Built a simulated A/B testing framework using chi-square significance testing to validate retention-intervention methodology on high-risk segments, integrated as an interactive tab in the live dashboard
PythonPandasScikit-learnLightGBM SHAPMLflowStreamlitPlotly MatplotlibSeabornSciPy
View case study
🏗 Architecture
Feature engineering feeds a LightGBM classifier trained with SMOTE-balanced data. Predictions route through a tuned decision threshold (0.35, chosen for recall), then into K-Means segmentation and SHAP explainability. MLflow tracks every experiment run, and the whole pipeline surfaces through a four-page Streamlit dashboard deployed on AWS EC2 behind Nginx.
⚠ Dataset Challenges
Only 26.6% of customers had actually churned, so a model optimizing for accuracy alone would just predict "stayed" for everyone. I addressed this with SMOTE oversampling and by tuning the classification threshold specifically to protect recall on churners rather than trusting the default 0.5 cutoff.
💡 What I Learned
Accuracy is often the wrong metric for imbalanced, business-critical problems — recall and explainability mattered more here. SHAP turned a "black box" score into reasons a retention team could act on, and deploying to EC2 myself taught me the ops side of ML that notebooks don't.
Medicine Recommendation System
GitHub Live Demo
41 diseases classified 132 symptom features
  • Built a Flask REST API serving real-time disease predictions from an SVM classifier trained across 41 diseases and 132 symptom features
  • Delivered personalized recommendations alongside each prediction — including precautions, medications, diet, and workout suggestions — to make model output directly actionable
  • Designed the full symptom-entry-to-results flow, from free-text input parsing to a categorized, interactive results panel
PythonFlaskScikit-learnPandasNumPy
View case study
🏗 Architecture
A Flask backend takes free-text or selected symptoms, vectorizes them into 132 binary features, and passes them to a trained SVM classifier covering 41 diseases. Each prediction is joined against lookup tables for description, precautions, medications, diet, and workout advice, then rendered as an interactive results panel. It's deployed on AWS EC2 behind Nginx at a dedicated path.
⚠ Dataset Challenges
Many diseases in the dataset shared overlapping symptom sets, which made naive classification ambiguous. I had to carefully encode the 132 symptom flags consistently, normalize inconsistent symptom naming, and evaluate per-class rather than relying on a single overall accuracy figure since some diseases were underrepresented.
💡 What I Learned
Building a recommendation layer on top of a classifier — not just predicting a label but linking it to actionable guidance — taught me to think about the full user journey. Migrating the live demo from Render/Streamlit Cloud to a self-hosted EC2 instance also taught me to fix cold-start latency and manage a reverse proxy myself.
Smart Inventory Management System
GitHub
  • Built a desktop inventory management system for product tracking and stock monitoring
  • Built a real-time dashboard with secure authentication, surfacing insights on sales performance, stock alerts, and category-level inventory
  • Added sales management features with automated billing calculations and data visualization for revenue analysis
  • Designed a custom Java Swing UI — including a dark-themed dashboard and custom dialog components in place of default Java dialogs — for a more polished, production-quality experience
Java (Swing)MySQLJDBCJFreeChart
View case study
🏗 Architecture
A Java Swing desktop client talks to a MySQL database over JDBC, with a dedicated screen per operation — add, update, delete, search, and sales entry — each backed by its own data-access logic. JFreeChart renders live revenue charts, and a custom FancyDialog component keeps confirmations and alerts consistent across the app.
⚠ Dataset Challenges
There was no public dataset to work from — I designed the relational schema (products, categories, sales, stock levels) from scratch. The harder problem was keeping inventory quantity and recorded sales consistent in real time and preventing duplicate or invalid entries at the database layer.
💡 What I Learned
This was my deepest dive into stateful GUI development and direct JDBC transaction handling outside a browser environment. It reinforced solid OOP structuring for a maintainable desktop app and gave me a much better feel for building a dashboard that stays in sync with a live database instead of static files.
